issue_comment_body: Probably fixed this in #32.

> Why isn't it already a unicode string if that is its encoding?

Because in Py2, a str is an equally reasonable way to pass text around :), but also because of what I mentioned about it not being too convenient to go decode all the string keys of a big nested object.

Because of this bug though that's exactly what I'm doing though, yes, and yes passing unicode objects into TOML does the correct thing.

> Is this an issue in Python3 too or only in Python2?

Haven't checked for sure, but from reading that code, almost certainly.

I can have a look at a PR if I get a minute here.

There is a test suite, but your [Travis config](https://github.com/uiri/toml/blob/master/.travis.yml) doesn't appear to run it, and I don't see clear instructions on testing it (I could probably figure it out from the test repo, but it would be nice to have brief instructions in this repo).
(Also python3-dev for p3...)

> I'll put all async code in a subfolder

That seems reasonable. I'd especially like it if there were symmetry with another package that served for non-async mode. For example, there's `irc.aio` and `irc.select` and each implements equivalent functionality. But that's not a prerequisite.

> some dependencies do not support async code

Ugh. Well, I guess that's an unfortunate situation, but not surprising. Probably best would be to make those features compatible with async as well. I'd be happy to accept those improvements as well. I would like them to be contributed back to the canonical project, all of which are open source (if they're not, that's a bug).

I see `Throttler` is imported from [jaraco.functools](https://pypi.org/project/jaraco.functools/), [found at GitHub](/jaraco/jaraco.functools).

If you wish to test with unreleased versions of jaraco.functools, it's easy to:

```
irc $ .tox/python/bin/pip install -e ../jaraco.functools
```

Then run tests or exercise the IRC library against the in-development version of jaraco.functools before committing/pushing/pull-requesting.

> Btw, I do think `wrapper=lambda x: x` makes things super clear... `wrapper=lambda sock: sock` is even better.

Yes, I agree. I think I was planning to move `identity` to another library (as I've used that pattern in more than a dozen places). It's the kind of thing that I feel should be in the stdlib, like `None` only for functions. But since it's not, it has to be explicit throughout the various implementations.

That said, there is a tension with `lambda`. Many Python developers, especially those unfamiliar with functional programming paradigms, find lambda objectionable. I'm perfectly comfortable with it and my sensibilities align with yours here. Just be aware that others may come along later and wish to change it (back).

In any case, sounds like you're on the right track.

Thanks but no thanks. I'm not sure I fully understand the implications of suppressing the exception and stopping iteration. For example, I imagine you could DoS the server by sending two undecodeable lines in each packet.

See the readme on the recommendations for how to handle encoding problems. The library provides clear interfaces on how to handle robustly any encoding situations.

As many times as users have encountered this situation, I would be inclined to change the default behavior from raising exceptions to replacing undecodeable characters, a la

```
irc.client.ServerConnection.buffer_class.errors = 'replace'
```

But one thing I don't want to do is alter the codebase in a way that silently suppresses errors without giving clients any control over it.

To expand a bit on that, by "awkward" I mean: (1) people aren't used to micro version requirements, (2) tooling isn't set up for it (e.g. a wheel can require 3.5.0-or-better, but it can't require 3.5.2-or-better), (3) I happen to be lagging a bit on CPython updates so curio's test suite keeps failing for me locally, which is what annoyed me into submitting this patch :-).

A problem I am seeing now is the following:

If I have a very deeply nested set of task running in pools, e.g. a generalized DAG dependency resolver as I am trying in the code [above](https://github.com/NNPDF/reportengine/blob/25f7b0c4680a91b692653e24b1a0b5f8c222dc99/src/reportengine/resourcebuilder.py#L135) I find no reasonable way of shutting down everything once I get an error in one of the tasks. After trying with many combinations of try except, the tasks scheduled with spawn seem to try to run in the pool before giving the opportunity to cancel them, creating a very long cascade of errors and unwanted work.

The workaround I have found is:

````
        wait_tasks = curio.wait(pending_tasks)
        async for completed_task in wait_tasks:
            try:
                result = await completed_task.join()
            except curio.TaskError as e:
                raise KeyboardInterrupt from e
````

which I believe works thanks to the special way in which Keyboard interrupt is treated now. Then this can be handled like:

````
     with kernel:
            try:
                kernel.run(main())
            except KeyboardInterrupt as e:
                log.info("Cancelling remaining tasks.")
                if e.__cause__:
                    raise e.__cause__
                raise
````

Could there be something like `curio.shutdown()` (or `raise curio.Shutdown()`) to handle this king of think?

Just a quick observation, as I've started to read (and then scanned)
through this thread at lunch.

1)  async/await is (my mind) _cooperative_ multitasking.

As such, I read this as if seeing cooperative- and preemptive- conflated.

2) Nick's example of I/o blocking behavior calls to mind the "context" lib
of golang (i.e. group timeout - read that as cooperative - behavior, a tool
for the convenience of the intentional programmer)

3) Nathaniel's concerns seem to be around externally controlling a
misbehaving cooperative- ...  Is suggest clearly separating the two
(cooperative & preemptive) and discussing them and addressing them
separately, distinctly.

4) because of this perspective, I find myself siding squarely with Dave
(keep it as is) and Imran - curio should not solve this, it should
rightfully be left in the hands of a competent cooperative-multitasking
developer to handle as appropriate;

5) because of Nick's notes, I think notes for managing inadvertently
triggering what Nathaniel is concerned with should be handled with
appropriately documenting ("this is cooperative-multitasking, and here's
what that implies...") and perhaps adding convenience libraries/classes for
programmers (or let others add), which is to say I fall squarely with Dave
and Imran (and through some cognitive translation, I imagine, Nick).

Caveat - I spent no more than lunchtime to scan this (but my impression
throughout has been consistent, so that's why I'm sharing).

Cheers,
Yarko

The PR will always represent the difference between my project/branch and yours. Whether you have two commits one day, five commits the next, or one the day after, your PR will always represent the whole, big-picture difference. Since you're only allowed to have one PR open at a time, there's no ambiguity. Most review systems work the same way.

Squashing contributions is generally considered good practice, anywhere. It's always going to be easier to wrap your head around third-party contributions when they're neatly packaged into single commits. It's also obviously perfectly acceptable to also keep whatever you have (if that helps you) and to just squash right before we're ready to merge, too.

Hi this is how dir_sync works. Keep in mind that I don't use it and has
been used only by a few users, so I'm not sure if it works in edge cases.

I've followed the official Microsoft docs, To be compliant with the docs I
added the LDAP_SERVER_DIRSYNC_OID control, the LDAP_SERVER_EXTENDED_DN_OID
control and the LDAP_SERVER_SHOW_DELETED_OID control.

I think the usage is quite simple:

to start a sync:
  sync = connection.extend.microsoft.dir_sync(root_partition)

to get the next batch of modifications:
  mods = []
  while sync.more_data:
      mods += sync.loop()

You can set all the options available in Microsoft docs for the DIrSync
 operation:
- sync_base: must be a root partition
- sync_filter : defaults to '(objectclass=*)'
- attributes: defaujlts to ALL_ATTRIBUTES
- cookie: you can leave this None or set a cookie from a previous loop.
- object_security: if True you get only object available to the user account,
default to False
- ancestors_first: if True you get all the containers objects before their
children, defaults to True
- public_data_only: if True you get data that don't need authorization, default
to False
- incremental_values: after the first loop you get only the modified
attributes,
defaults to True
- max_length: the max number of bytes that could be returned, defauilt to
2147483647 (the max possible value). The actual number of bytes returned is
constrained by the server too
- hex_guid: If True returns GUID in hex form. defaults to False.

As per Microsoft specifications the DN of each object has also the GUID
(and SID if available) information.

You can aso add the LDAP_SERVER_EXTENDED_DN_OID and
LDAP_SERVER_SHOW_DELETED_OID
to your other searches.

For some usage example look at \ldap3\test\testMicrosoftAD.py

Please give me feedback and suggestion on how this function can be improved.

Bye,
Giovanni

The argument is actually much more subtle than you say.

If you're varying a single detection threshold (ie, for precision) while holding the true positives constant, then yes, the limit goes to 1.  In this case, mir_eval and sklearn do the right thing by default.

However, the case we're talking about here involves taking both the predicted and true positives to 0, which is not covered by your argument.  More precisely, we'd have to compute `lim_{tp, pp -> 0} pp/tp`, which is not a well-behaved sequence (ie, not bounded above), and monotone convergence doesn't apply. (I think; I'd have to consult a measure theory textbook...)

If you want to break it up into two limits, you get either
```
lim_{pp->0} lim_{tp->0} pp/tp
```
or
```
lim_{tp->0} lim_{pp->0} pp/tp
```
These limits are not interchangeable, and give different answers.  (Note that it's either 0 or +inf; 1 is not one of them.)

Hello there and thanks for putting this up. To be honest this doesn't strike me as something particularly useful, consistent or easily portable across platforms. For instance:

- `flags` really isn't useful unless also some constants are exposed (which ones? where are they defined? what do they represent? where are they documented?)
- `path` being an integer is kinda weird, as one would expect a string (a minor thing, just mentioning)
- `open_fds` should at least return a `type`, otherwise it's kinda useless. To quote your example:

```
In [9]: python._proc.open_fds()
Out[9]:
[popenfile(path=121124880, fd=0, position=0, mode='r', flags=0),          # FD0 (STDIN),  pipe #121124880
 popenfile(path='/dev/pts/5', fd=1, position=0, mode='r+', flags=32770),  # FD1 (STDOUT), special character (tty) on pts/5
```

...you inferred that the first element is a pipe and the second is a "special" but it's should be the lib which tells you that (also what's a "special" fd?). That poses the disturbing question: what are the available fd types and what do they represent? Are there just "special", "pipe" and "file" or also others? To my knowledge there are many others: fifos, sockets, char and block devices, open directories and who knows what else. Should they be returned? Also, each fd type represents a completely different thing. How should they be exposed to the end user?

A utility which managed to wrap this all up is `lsof`, but that's a cmdline utility which just prints stuff on screen. A lib is something different: you have to think carefully about an API which is consistent across all possible types and which is usable, which is far from easy if possible at all.

But `NoBrokersAvailable` is raised from the version probing, not the bootstrap process itself. It is true that we enable version probing by default, but if you configure an explicit `api_version` then you would not get this exception and you would just have a non-functional consumer. Perhaps this is what @Ormod is seeing?

But if this does occur then `consumer.poll` should not hang indefinitely. It should still respect the timeout_ms parameter. So I'm still a little confused about the underlying issue.

Stepping back, however, when bootstrapping does fail it is because either (1) the nodes configured for bootstrapping are down temporarily -- a cluster failure, or maintenance, etc. In these cases the error likely requires manual intervention at the broker, but should not require any changes at the consumer. So we (kafka client) can just carry on with periodic retries until the nodes come back and we can start working again. This is the current behavior. But (2) if the client is just misconfigured -- either the hosts dont exist, or dns is wrong, or some networking issue prevents a connection -- then there is no future recovery without manual intervention on the client side. In this case users would probably benefit from a fast failure. But how to tell the difference between 1 and 2 ? I'm not sure there is any way to do that. Maybe we could add an option that would change the working assumption that all bootstrap errors are broker issues (no exception raised) to assume that all bootstrap errors are client errors (always raise exception).

I think there is some misreading here. Nobody said that:

> Originally you said the problem is because my strategy didn't prepare for the fact that next could be called multiple time with the same data. Well -- nothing can be more prepared for multiple invocations than a one-time flag:

what was said was:

> In any case I guess (and this is only a guess) that you may be looking for replaying. Replaying delivers always and up to date 10 minutes bar. But you have to take into account that your next method will be called several times with the same len.

But let's focus:

  - There isn't a lookahead bias here. There is a lookback bias in place

The order is being executed in the larger timeframe order which is ticking slower than the smaller timeframe. In every tick of the smaller timeframe, the same bar of the larger timeframe is seen and the broker executes it.

The broker doesn't know the larger timeframe data is slowed (frozen) by the slower timeframe data which is ticking faster.

A similar case is if you simply replay a data feed. The broker will be presented with a data feed which has *length* frozen in time for a while (the bar values change, but the *length* doesn't). To support such a case the broker uses the latest `tick_open` to execute.

One of the reasons why the smaller timeframe has to be inserted.

Can this be changed? Yes and no.

In a replay scenario, the datetime will not compulsory change with the delivery of each tick. Platforms tend to offer millisecond resolution and several ticks can for sure happen within a millisecond. The broker is then bound to execute with the next incoming tick without looking at the datetime.

This means that in the use case laid out above (smaller timeframe, larger timeframe, let's buy from the larger timeframe), the larger timeframe whose *length* is frozen will offer the wrong *tick* to the broker.

The only thing that could be added is a parameter to the broker, to activate a control check and only execute orders if the datetime has advanced.

This is documented here:

[Docs - DateTime Management](https://www.backtrader.com/docu/timemgmt.html)

> The end user can override and determine the actual timezone for the output

>  - Providing a tz parameter to the data source. This must be an object compatible with the datetime.tzinfo interface. Most likely the user will provide a pytz.timezone instance

This was extended in the case of `IBData` because the platform provides timezone information and 
`IBData` makes every possible effort to create a *timezone* object even locally importing `pytz`.

In the case of the `GenericCSVData` the expectation is to pass a `pytz` compatible object.

``findowner`` traverses the Python frame stack to find a specific class instance using either the Python convention of using ``self`` or the internal ``backtrader`` convention of using ``_obj`` in metaclasses

That's how, for example, any ``Indicator`` derived class knows in which ``Strategy`` instance it has bee instantiated and can register behind the scenes. That allows the strategy instance to later kick the indicators in order to perform its calculations.

This removes burden from the end users of the platform, because no "register to parent" calls are needed (which also implies having a ``parent`` argument passed during instantiation)

``_obj.__OwnerCls`` may be defined by the classes to restrict the type of instances to find. If no definition is made the first found instace of ``LineMultiple`` will be a match.

A definition of ``_OwnerCls`` can be seen in ``observer.py`` where it looks like: ``_OwnerCls = StrategyBase``

These classes and functions are not meant to be documented. They are the internals of the platform and have suffered several changes and reordering and may suffer more.

``LineRoot`` is a modern addition to unify all classes carrying lines, be it 1 or be it more, which aided in simplifying some other parts of the internals.

Hey, FYI, don't play with it too much.  So far I've changed the base gemetries to subclasses of ndarray, added cacheable properties to base geometries, and changed the static and cacheable properties to be properties rather than dictionary items.  so `normalize(segs1['begin'].arr)` is now `segs1.begin.normal`.  Subcalssing ndarray is kind of a pain, but it's really the way to do it I think, and I'm just trying to figure out how slices should behave before pushing my changes.  

I think slices should be invalid.  Otherwise, you spend more time in python land creating a sliced object that has sliced cahced properties (if they've been cached yet) without knowing that you are causing this overhead.  This would be bad for calculating results and such where you just want to operate on raw arrays.  There will likely be a downcast method (`raw()`) that returns a plain ndarray of the data (for when you're slicing to perform some operations on the data, like in a result calculation) and If you want a Line2d object that is a slice of another you can easily create a new one from the ndarray slice and will know that you've invalidated the cache.  ie: `newline2d = Line2d(begin=oldline2d.begin.raw()[mask], normal=oldline2d.normal.raw()[mask])`.

You're still creating a new array when you call raw to get ready to do slices (instead of getting a memory view), but in performace critical sections, one can probably reuse these arrays in calculating the results anyways.

Though I went through a related investigation before I started PyEasyArchive to determine how to manage 7Z files from Python and only ended-up with *libarchive*, I did another check just now. There still appears to be virtually no library support for reading 7Z (unencrypted, at all). The `7z` tool build-process only produces a bunch of object-files and a handful of executables.

It seems like there's no practical interest in supporting cryptography directly in archives. I've recently become a committed fan of XZ (which is LZMA/LZMA 2, and essentially a simpler version of 7Z with an interface similar to GZ and BZ). It's a fairly new implementation, I believe, and, even there, there's zero support for encryption. Unlike 7Z, it does properly support Unix file-ownership, though. Also, unlike 7Z, it's natively supported in Python in the [lzma](https://docs.python.org/3/library/lzma.html) package as of 3.3 .

If you have any choice in the matter, you might consider using XZ in a TAR -> COMPRESS -> ENCRYPT process. It would greatly simplify your efforts and remove your dependency on what looks to be a decreasingly-popular format (keeping in mind that I have loved 7Z and, until recently, have been using it nearly exclusively for nine-years). You could use the [tarfile](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tarfile.html) package to produce a TAR file, push it through the LZMA package to render an XZ, and then encrypt (naming it something like "xze"). Because of the order of the steps, above, you can also pipe the output of the decryption directly into TAR (using 'xJ') to decompress by hand.

Hi @khavishbhundoo the README has some examples on how to use the library in general. If the files are small it will work really well just following that pattern and writing the response contents out to files once they're finished. 

If the files are large however you'll be outside of the designed use-case of requests futures which is intended more for API/web requests with relatively small payloads that fit in memory. In this case you'd probably be better off writing something a bit custom. I threw the following together to test and see if my first thought at what that might look like would work and it seems to:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
from requests import Session
from time import time

url = 'https://github.com/django/django/archive/2.0.2.tar.gz'
executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4)
session = Session()


def log(msg):
    print('{}: {}'.format(time(), msg))


def download(url, filename):
    log('request {}'.format(filename))
    resp = session.get(url, stream=True)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    with open(filename, 'wb') as fh:
        for chunk in resp.iter_content(chunk_size=1024):
            log('chunk {}'.format(filename))
            fh.write(chunk)
    log('done {}'.format(filename))
    return filename

log('begin')
futures = []
for i in range(4):
    log('start {}'.format(i))
    future = executor.submit(download, url, '/tmp/download-{}.tar.gz'.format(i))
    futures.append(future)
filenames = [f.result() for f in futures]
log('end')
```

That's really noisy since it shows every 1024 byte chunk coming down, but the intention was to show that multiple files are streaming at once and complete asynchronously

    def _to_timestamp(self, timestamp_str):
        """Converts a timestamp string as returned by the API to
        a native datetime object and return it."""
        timestamp_str = timestamp_str[:-3]
        timestamp_str += '00'
        return datetime.datetime.strptime(timestamp_str, '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z')


All then worked for me, but I’m not sure if that is correct for other OS’es.  It’s the first time I’ve been looking at python in 5 years, so a little rusty.

For some reason ("Unfortunately, we don’t support that file type."… But it's a ZIP file?), I cannot attach the code. So, here it is in all its gorey details:

```python
import pytest
from django.db import models
from dirtyfields import DirtyFieldsMixin


class Location(DirtyFieldsMixin, models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=128)


class Assembly(DirtyFieldsMixin, models.Model):
    contained_in = models.ForeignKey('self', null=True, blank=True)
    locality = models.ForeignKey(Location, null=True, blank=True)

    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Additions to the default abstract save method."""
        try:
            # import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()
            if self.is_dirty(check_relationship=True):
                dirties = self.get_dirty_fields(check_relationship=True)
                if 'locality' in dirties.keys():
                    self.ripple_downwards('locality', self.locality)
        finally:
            super(Assembly, self).save(*args, **kwargs)

    def ripple_downwards(self, key, value):
        """Sets key to value in all sub assemblies in a recursive way.

        :param String key: A model field.
        :param object value: An object those model's field can be set to.

        """
        for item in self.assembly_set.all():
            setattr(item, key, value)
            item.save()
            item.ripple_downwards(key, value)


@pytest.mark.django_db
def test_ripple_down():
    sut = Assembly.objects.create()
    sub = Assembly.objects.create()
    sub.contained_in = sut

    local = Location.objects.create(name='Afghanistan')
    sut.locality = local
    sut.save()  # This should cause the Location to be passed downwards.
    sut.refresh_from_db()

    sub.refresh_from_db()
    assert local == sub.locality
```

You can customize filters using the LDAP_AUTH_FORMAT_SEARCH_FILTERS setting.
The setting takes a path to a function that is used to compile the LDAP search filters.

The default implementation is here:
https://github.com/etianen/django-python3-ldap/blob/master/django_python3_ldap/utils.py#L116

You will probably want to define your own function that calls the default implementation, and adds your own filters:

``` py
# settings.py

LDAP_AUTH_FORMAT_SEARCH_FILTERS = "your_app.ldap.format_search_filters"

# your_app/ldap.py

from django_python3_ldap.utils import format_search_filters as format_search_filters_base

def format_search_filters(ldap_fields):
    search_filters = format_search_filters_base(ldap_fields)
    search_filters.append("YOUR ADDITIONAL FILTER HERE")
    return search_filteres
```

For me, I have identified the problem code as being in method choice() of class MPTTModelChoiceIterator within the module zinnia/admin/fields.py.

The zinnia code overloads the choice method of ModelChoiceIterator from django.forms.models. Unfortunately this overload returns a tuple that django chokes on.

Specifically, this next line seems wrong:

   ` return super(MPTTModelChoiceIterator, self).choice(obj) + ((tree_id, left),)`

A better solution would be something like:
```
    choice = super(MPTTModelChoiceIterator, self).choice(obj)
    return choice (choice[:1] + (tree_id, left),), choice[1]
```
Note the sorting information should be grouped into the first inner tuple. Making this change to the source worked for me but I have not extensively tested it. Hope it helps someone.

The assertion error is because Django 1.10 no longer supports this way of assigning model managers. It introduced a new API instead, using [default_manager_name](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/models/options/#django.db.models.Options.default_manager_name) meta option (and an equivalent for base manager name). $ bower list --paths
```
$ bower list --paths
```

Output is

```
  'roboto-fontface': [
    'bower_components/roboto-fontface/css/*.*',
    'bower_components/roboto-fontface/fonts/*.*'
  ]
```

So bower itself is not resolving the globs. Sorry, but I would like to have the bower main array to contain an exact list of files, that one has to include into his project (as part of a bundling step or so) to use this fontface. The maintainence of this explicit file list is not nice, I know, but I does not change often anyway.

I will close this PR for now. Feel free to reopen, if you disagree.

`import shutil
import lxml.etree as et

# The integration of pytest plugin junitxml and python module 'flaky'
# give rise to 'extra' testcases in the result junit-xml file.
#
# This function is a workaround to remove these 'extras'
def delete_flaky_reruns(junitxml_file):
    doc = et.parse(junitxml_file)
    suite = doc.getroot()
    for case in suite:
        try:
            # If the case lack name it is added because of flaky rerun
            name = case.attrib['name']
        except KeyError:
            print "Removing faulty testcase"
            case.getparent().remove(case)

    doc.write(junitxml_file)`

Have a look at this unittest that can help on understand the problem:
https://gist.github.com/5ca405dabb6c0563c892.git
Two considerations:
1. The [output problem](https://gist.github.com/manuelep/5ca405dabb6c0563c892#file-test_validate_and_update_or_insert_with_json_field-py-L51) comes out adding the validator IS_JSON and web2py do it for you by default.
2. To avoid the unwanted behaviour to need json object serialization is necessary to remove the IS_JSON validator.

So at the moment the best way to by-pass the bug is to remove (at least just before calling the validate_and_update_or_insert method) the json field default validator.

This change was intentional. One big win of #1182 was that global and local parameters were merged. There's no difference between `--MyTask-param2` and `--param2` any more. The former will always work (in case of conflicts) while `--param2` is shorter and more convenient, otherwise there's no difference.

I suppose that we could reintroduce the old behaviour without needing to rewrite everything, but then the parsing logic would start to become complex again. 

@daveFNbuck, Don't you like that you can override variables in tasks too? It have been convenient for me a few times. Currently there's no distinction between Config classes and Tasks, which is pretty nice from an implementers point of view as well. :)

Have there been any real world use cases that's affected (negatively) by this?

Oftentimes importers cannot tell whether the texture being imported is actually a height vs a normal map. Oftentimes formats do not specify this (OBJ for example does not, at least not in common use).

The original distinction drawn by the materials API is thus kind of obsolete. If you have control over your art/content pipeline, establish some convention for textures and ignore the type hint (i.e. treat both equally). If you have to deal with arbitrary inputs, consider guessing by looking at the pixels and see if they're all grayscale :-)

A similar issue is with object space normal maps vs. tangent space normal maps. Luckily, the former are used so rarely.

This isn't public currently because it's specific to the botocore implementation of pagination and it differs from how the auto-pagination works in other SDKs (ruby, go, etc. they only offer page iteration). Botocore is a little different here because it powers the AWS CLI which has it's own special requirements for pagination (result key aggregation). 

I also think the interface for this function is a little strange. Ideally, I think we would support item level iteration with an interface like this maybe:

```python
# Create a reusable Paginator
paginator = client.get_paginator('list_objects')

for item in paginator.paginate(Bucket='my-bucket').items_iterator():
    # gives you each item in the default result_key (`Contents`).
    print(item)
```

And if there were multiple paginated result keys the `items_iterator` could take an optional `result_key` param that defaults to the default result key.

Either way, marking this as a feature request for item level pagination.

If you are going to implement that function, what’s preventing you from writing an actual FWTask returning that FWAction in the first place? The overhead is minimal, and you can do much more with that than using PyTask.

PyTask, though powerful, was never meant to supplant the writing of FWTasks. It is really meant for the execution of one liner Python functions. If someone really needs to handle complexity higher than just executing a one-liner python function and storing that output, he really should be writing a FWTask.

What's happening is that `persistent` respects the PURE_PYTHON environment variable even after it's installed:

```python
# persistent/__init__.py
PURE_PYTHON = os.environ.get('PURE_PYTHON')
if not PURE_PYTHON:
    try:
        from persistent.cPersistence import Persistent
```

`zope.container.contained`, however, did not, only at build time:
```python
try:
    from zope.container._zope_container_contained import ContainedProxyBase
    from zope.container._zope_container_contained import getProxiedObject
    from zope.container._zope_container_contained import setProxiedObject
except ImportError: # PyPy
    from zope.container._proxy import py_getProxiedObject as getProxiedObject
    from zope.container._proxy import py_setProxiedObject as setProxiedObject
    from zope.container._proxy import PyContainedProxyBase as ContainedProxyBase

```

 With the `usedevelop = true` that's needed for coverage to work, if you happened to have a `_zope_container_contained.so` (platform specific) laying around in `src/zope/container/` then that would still be imported...leading to a mismatch. (I happened to have one from October 2016 still for some reason.)

Distributing binary wheels on PyPI probably exacerbates this sort of problem.

This can be fixed pretty easily in contained.py by doing the same thing that `persistent` does:

```python
from zope.container._proxy import py_getProxiedObject as getProxiedObject
from zope.container._proxy import py_setProxiedObject as setProxiedObject
from zope.container._proxy import PyContainedProxyBase as ContainedProxyBase
if not os.getenv('PURE_PYTHON'):
    try:
        from zope.container._zope_container_contained import ContainedProxyBase
    except ImportError: # PyPy
        pass
    else:
        from zope.container._zope_container_contained import getProxiedObject
        from zope.container._zope_container_contained import setProxiedObject
```

thanks very much Eleftherios for the detail. This decision making falls in the category "common sense". You average the scores with a norm in a kind of loss-function (see optimization) in order to decide which reconstruction procedure is better. You might even weight the scores if you have favourite features. Then you say: the reconstruction procedure with the best overall score wins the race. There is nothing wrong with that, you want to arrive to a decision somehow.  Only take care not to perceive or sell this “overall score” as an “average ks-distance”, since there are no distributions associated with it. The ks-distance is the maximum distance between two empirical cumulative distribution functions. For the “overall-score” we do not have any distributions associated with it. 
Summarising: What you propose is a sensible heuristic to help you decide between reconstructing procedures and systems. It’s like evaluating a loss-functional for optimization. It lacks statistical meaning, since it does not have any associated distributions like the ks-distance has. But it’s nothing wrong with this as long as you are clear for yourself and make it clear to your clients. Use a formulation like “we build an overall score for the reconstruction process using the ks-distances of the feature distributions”. 
I hope I made myself clear, just ask if you have questions. This reminds me the difference between statistical and practical significance a bit.

In Font Awesome 5.0.x, if you want to `require` the whole icon set, it should look like this:
```javascript
const fas = require('@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-solid')['default']
```
(notice the `['default']` on the end, or it could be `.default`)
If you [look here](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/blob/31281606f5205b0191c17c3b4d2d56e1ddbb2dc6/advanced-options/use-with-node-js/fontawesome-free-solid/index.js#L1127) at `@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-solid/index.js`, you'll see that we have an export on the `default` key that is just the icon set, and it's in the structure expected by `library.add()`.

If you do the `require()` without the `['default']` at the end, you're getting the whole `exports` object which is not the expected structure for `library.add()`.

[Here's a working demo in a repo](https://github.com/mlwilkerson/demo-require-library-add-whole-icon-pack/) that `require()`s a whole icon set, adds it to the library and reads something back out.

That demo repo has two branches, because the way we export stuff is different in our current 5.0.x releases from what our 5.1.x branch will look like once released (currently in pre-release). So its  `master` branch is for the 5.1 approach, and the `5.0-dependencies` branch is for the for the 5.0.x approach (which I assume is what matters for the situations posted above).

@bschwartz757 I'm curious to know what difference it would make if you made two changes to what you originally posted:

1. Add the `['default']` to your `require()`.
1. Call `library.add()` only once, passing in everything together as a comma separate list of args.
Like this:
```javascript
var fontawesome = require('@fortawesome/fontawesome')
var faBrands = require('@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-brands')['default']
var faSolid = require('@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-solid')['default']
var faRegular = require('@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-regular')['default']
fontawesome.library.add(faBrands, faSolid, faRegular)
```

Here is the reason. The lock is inside the lower level [cache.remove()](https://github.com/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/compare/threadsafe-cache-pop?expand=1#diff-68ac7ae8069c0f8a36f5842ed721b98bL80), that lock has NOT been "lifted". However, it is the higher level [_refresh_entry_if_necessary()](https://github.com/AzureAD/azure-activedirectory-library-for-python/blob/0.5.0/adal/cache_driver.py#L166) uses this pattern "search cached token; oh it is stale, let's remove it", and that search-and-then-delete logic is not within a lock. I do have something in mind and plan to rewrite this cache logic completely in the future, but for now, the proposing patch is good enough. And nope, it won't mask any other exceptions.

I'll include that patch into the upcoming release.

tl;dr if you're catching the exception, use the broader `OSError` and discover _why_ it happened yourself.  Error code returned in `e.errno` is filesystem-dependent and unreliable.

Python invokes system calls, in the case of `os.rename()`, that's `rename(2)` (see `man 2 rename`). This will trigger the behavior that `os.rename()` does:
```
mkdir from to
touch from/contents to/contents
strace mv -T from to
```

`OSError` will reflect in its `errno ` attribute whatever the operating system (kernel) reports back when the C library invokes `rename(2)`, but this is filesystem-dependent. In the case of NFS on CentOS 5, or XFS on CentOS 7 running a stock kernel, it can be `EEXIST`, which is error 17.
```
lstat("from", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=4096, ...}) = 0
lstat("to", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=4096, ...}) = 0
rename("from", "to")                    = -1 EEXIST (File exists)
```

`ext3` on CentOS 5 running kernel 3.10, may raise `ENOTEMPTY` (error 39), like `util.py` catches: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/ba58a854d561dcf6963c0097cad544bfe2861c5a/pex/util.py#L177
```
lstat("from", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=4096, ...}) = 0
lstat("to", {st_mode=S_IFDIR|0755, st_size=4096, ...}) = 0
rename("from", "to")                    = -1 ENOTEMPTY (Directory not empty)
```

Anyway, you'll want a more robust approach that is kernel and filesystem agnostic.

I have been thinking about this, and from the conversation above it sounds like there are two use cases that need to be supported:
1) Remove a Rule from an environment, to be used for cleaning up after automated testing of a single Rule or cost reduction
2) Scrub an AWS Account clean of RDK-deployed resources and configuration to clean up after automated testing of end-to-end deployment automation.

I think what's needed is actually two new commands:
1) "undeploy" which removes a deployed rule that has been deployed the "classic" way without using --functions-only.  This would simply delete the CFN template for the specific Rule and would be used for testing individual rules.
2) "clean" which would completely remove all Rules, as well as remove any RDK-managed S3 buckets and IAM resources, as well as the configuration recorder and delivery channel.  This would be used for testing more complex CI/CD pipelines and multi-account deployments, and would satisfy jongogo's added requirements from July 17th.

If that all makes sense I can split off a second issue for the `clean` command.

@Zulko I did a speed test using the below code. Using this branch of Imageio and your stable moviepy both writers are about the same speed. The Imageio is a 10% faster but that is probably due to the difference in default quality of the variable bitrate between the two libraries.

Here are the results:
````
Encoding 5 videos with 100 frames, each (512, 512, 3) pixels
imageio encoding time 23.549 sec
moviepy encoding time 25.7 sec
moviepy encoding time / imageio encoding time: 1.1
````

Here is the code I used:
```python
import numpy as np
import imageio
import moviepy.video.io.ffmpeg_writer as moviepy_ffmpeg
import time

img = imageio.imread("lena.png")
movie_size = img.shape

n_movies = 5
n_frames = 100
fps=30

print "Encoding {} videos with {} frames, each {} pixels".format(n_movies, n_frames, movie_size)
def add_frames(frame_writer_func):
    # Fade in image
    for index in range(n_frames):
        frame_writer_func((img*(1.0*index/n_frames)).astype(np.uint8))
    # Frames with noise
    for index in range(n_frames):
        ar = np.random.randint(0, 255, movie_size).astype(np.uint8)
        frame_writer_func(ar)

start = time.time()
for i in range(n_movies):
    writer = imageio.get_writer('imageio_video.mp4', fps=fps)
    add_frames(writer.append_data)
    writer.close()
imageio_dur = time.time() - start
print "imageio encoding time {:.5} sec".format(imageio_dur)

start = time.time()
for i in range(n_movies):
    writer = moviepy_ffmpeg.FFMPEG_VideoWriter('moviepy_video.mp4', movie_size[:2], fps)
    add_frames(writer.write_frame)
    writer.close()
moviepy_dur = time.time() - start
print "moviepy encoding time {:.5} sec".format(moviepy_dur)
print "moviepy encoding time / imageio encoding time: {:.2}".format(moviepy_dur/imageio_dur)
```

If it's empty, and you've set it to use SSL, then it uses the certificates provided by the `certifi` Python module\*, which is a lumped bundle of the certificates recognized by Mozilla in Python module form.  In such a case, it should guarantee that most commercially signed certificates will be recognized.  It does not use the OS certificate bundle.

`certificate` ostensibly should be able to recognize remote keys, where as `client_key` and `client_cert` imply the means by which a remote entity can verify Curator is trusted.

`ssl_no_validate` is really only useful for people who use self-signed certificates and can't be bothered to actually use the public key/certificate they've created as the `certificate`, so as to ensure that secure communications are validated.  It will do the PSK thing, but won't validate that the remote host is recognized by `certificate`.  So, you have _encrypted_ but not validated communications.


\* If you've installed a DEB/RPM or Windows package, this is provided.  If you're installing via pip, you need to install it yourself.

The reason you are getting 4 when querying is because the class initializes the field to 4, and the xml deserializer doesn't see the xml element in the response, so it doesn't touch the field, leaving it set to 4.

It would be better to have the class initialize the field to 4, except for the fact that the deserializer looks at the (default) value of each field to determine what type each field is.  If it's set to None, it doesn't know so it stores it as a string.  Not that great either.

I think what you should do in this case is set the value to None when creating the endpoint, and ignoring the value when querying, since it doesn't apply for udp.

@asvetlov a bit more context. They end point providing the websocket is guarded by authorization layer, which can stop the handshake before the ws protcol handlers are invoked. It returns different errors messages for the various authorization errors. 
In our e2e tests which use the aiohttp client to connect to the server we need to expose these errors to properly log and report them. Developers otherwise have a hard time analyzing the situation.


Regarding partial response:
For proper reporting we would need the complete error.

It is quite normal for an http error to return a response body. ( sometimes json to offer structured error data) . I dont see why websockets on protocol level are a special case during the handshake phase.

Thinking a bit : the method Ws_connect is a bit different in its interface than its counter parts (get, post) : it doesnt return the actual response and doesnt offer raise_for_status.

The abstraction it provides is a bit too much for the error reporting case. 
I think there two possible solutions to remove the mismatch:
1. An optional call back to let the caller process the response fully in the error case
2. New ws_connect variant. Return a response object. And that offers a method to access the websocket interface.

Hi,

I am seeing the same error messages. In our setup we are serving notebooks from a Samba share, and I am pretty sure that is the root cause of the error. (To be precise we are running Jupyter in a Docker container, based on the official Docker images but heavily customised, and the share is a [File Storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/files/) on Azure, which is mounted using the SMB3 protocol on the Linux host, so it is quite complicated, but I think most of that is not related.)

I have checked the developer tools in my browser and found the debug message recently added to Jupyter:
```
Last saving was done on `Mon Feb 15 2016 20:55:38 GMT+0100 (CET)`
(save-success:2016-02-15T19:55:38.909885+00:00), 
while the current file seem to have been saved on 
`2016-02-15T19:55:38.977767+00:00`
```
As you can see there is really only a very small difference, a fraction of a second, and it definitely seems to be a timestamp issue between the host and the SMB server.

Similar issues have been reported for numerous tools over the years, including [Eclipse](https://bugs.eclipse.org/bugs/show_bug.cgi?id=137011), [Gedit](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gedit/+bug/34813), [Emacs](https://bugzilla.samba.org/show_bug.cgi?id=11078) and [Samba](https://bugzilla.samba.org/show_bug.cgi?id=4076) itself [multiple times](https://bugzilla.samba.org/show_bug.cgi?id=6925), and you can find references to Samba shares and also VirtualBox shares in the comments. 

As far as I can see in the linked bug reports some tools are still unfixed, while others (most notably Eclipse) managed to work around this by changing how they check for changes. Is there a chance a similar change can be applied in Jupyter without breaking existing functionality for everyone else?

Ok, I've figured out what's going wrong here. The SafeNet server is sending back two pieces of information when you make a QUERY_SERVER_INFORMATION request: the server information string itself (which you listed above in your initial post) and a vendor identification string (which turns out here to just be: SafeNet, Inc.). This is expected behavior. For this case, both pieces of data are encoded in UTF-8, which is also expected. (Note: there's a minor issue with how the server information string itself is encoded but it isn't causing the error we're dealing with here).

This is the real issue. The KMIP spec dictates that the vendor identification string should be encoded before the server information data in the response payload, but SafeNet is encoding it after the server information data. The PyKMIP client strictly follows the spec and checks for vendor identification data before checking for server information data. As a result, the client reaches the 24 bytes of vendor identification data after decoding the server information and sees it as extra, unexpected information associated with the server information data block and breaks.

TL;DR This is a problem with the SafeNet appliance not strictly following the KMIP spec when encoding the server information and vendor identification data in the response payload for a QUERY_SERVER_INFORMATION request.

Handling custom attributes is more complicated than that. Both the PyKMIP client and the server (PyKMIP, Thales, etc) will need to know how to process the custom attribute at the byte level, whether it's a data blob, a set of integers, a string, etc. You can't just pass arbitrary values to the client and expect it to send them out over the wire; it needs to know more about each value, its type, and its composition. Your server will need to know how to do comparisons with the attribute value if you want to use it with a `locate` filter.

Is there documentation from Thales on the specification for this custom attribute? It should look like specification details from the KMIP specification. Is that documentation publicly available (I'd be interested in taking a look)?

As for `other_attributes`, it is too open ended for the API. I may be ok with something like `custom_attributes`, since that's allowed in the specification, but again, I need more information.

@AlyssaDaemon Sorry, just had to check; sort of like the IT question "have you tried turning it off and on again?".

I can't think of any reason why the presence of Mono would affect loading that library.  It needs

* libcrypto.1.0.0.dylib, from rpath
* libssl.1.0.0.dylib, from rpath
* /usr/lib/libc++.1.dylib
* /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib

The first two dependencies should be met by brew install/link of the OpenSSL package.  The second two should just be there on the Mac.

The library asserts that in addition to the standard rpath it includes loader_path; so it will first try the directory the library itself is in before falling back to system probing.

While the default probing path is $HOME/lib;/usr/local/lib;/usr/lib, it can be altered by some environment variables.  See https://developer.apple.com/library/mac/documentation/DeveloperTools/Conceptual/DynamicLibraries/100-Articles/DynamicLibraryUsageGuidelines.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40001928-SW21 for more information.

The only things that I can think of at this point are:
* Check that the /usr/local/lib symlinks are valid
* Check that you don't have any of the rpath-changing environment variables defined (or, that if you do, you try unsetting them to diagnose the problem)

You're the first person who has reported that `brew link --force openssl` didn't fix everything for them, so I'm at a bit of a loss, advicewise.

You need the english name because the regkey has always a english name, the localized is for displaying only.

Currently `winreg` tires to open because it uses the localized `keydict["StandardName"]`:

`SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Time Zones\Mitteleuropäische Zeit`

but that regkey doesn't exists. The name is always english `keydict["TimeZoneKeyName"]` (Windows 7+): 
`SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Time Zones\W. Europe Standard Time`

so there is no unicode issue even, so there is no real `winreg` issue, since we never need to open a unicode regkey (because it doesn'r exists)

You can keep the local name in `self._std_abbr` and just change:

```python
tzkeyname = text_type('{kn}\\{sn}').format(kn=TZKEYNAME,
                                                          sn=self._std_abbr)
```
to:
```python
tzkeyname = text_type('{kn}\\{sn}').format(kn=TZKEYNAME,
                                                          sn=keydict["TimeZoneKeyName"])
```
For pre Windows 7 you need to find the english name just like in the linked tzlocal file by looping trough all `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Time Zones\`  subkeys and compare the `Std` value to the `keydict["StandardName"]` value.

Just to clarify, there are two points at stake here:

- the whitelist feature, which is what the OP is about,
- the naming of load_only/dump_only (side note in the OP)

Regarding the second point, there are two potentially surprising things about those names:

As @nickw444 said in OP

> Also to note, the naming of load_only feels like a misnomer without reading the docs in detail - If you specify a field in load_only, it will only be available for loading, not dumping - but taking another perspective to this could imply that it's an iterable of fields that are whitelisted to be loaded, but can still be dumped.

And as @deckar01 said

> A good way to illustrate why this is a strange interface is to create a schema with a field that is both load_only and dump_only

I found this a bit surprising and so did my colleagues.

As I said, it is no big deal. You get hit once, and then you understand and you know. It might not be worth the pain of changing load_only/dump_only in the whole library (and all the codes using it). But since it is a breaking change (mitigated by keeping old terms working although considered deprecated), the release of v3 would be a good time to do it.

I don't mean to argue about this. I don't really mind, that was just a suggestion. And the "not worth the pain" argument is perfectly relevant.

The current behavior is exactly what you mentioned; everything is implicitly castable to string.  And I agree with you -- I think it should be either strict or follow the semantics of to<...>(), not somewhere in between. 

The weird behavior in question is due to the following use case.  The Levenstein distance is exposed directly through the extensions mechanism as 

`double levenshtein(const std::string& a, std::string& b);`

The test that's failing simply calls this function with a list and a dict types and asserts that an exception is raised -- i.e. the previous behavior here raised an error; the new mechanism does not.  

I suspect that what changed is the converter did actually change behavior in this, but the implicit casting behavior went through the flex_list converter method and showed up only here.  Thus the old converter didn't match the behavior of the class and now it does.

There are trade-offs to be made here. Results in `py-linq` can be consumed more than once because it caches (*i.e.* takes a copy of!) the source data into an internal list (possibly multiple times!). This is obviously hugely problematic for very large or unbounded data series, which `asq` will handle without problems.

First of all, notice that `py_linq` isn't re-executing the query, it's just regurgitating the results it gave you last time:

```
>>> from py_linq import Enumerable
>>> a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
>>> res = Enumerable(a).where(lambda x: x>2)
>>> print(list(res))
[3, 4, 5, 6]
>>> a.append(7)
>>> a
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
>>> print(list(res))
[3, 4, 5, 6]
```

Now let's look at memory usage on identical queries. First `py_linq`:

```
>>> # 3.5 MB
>>> from py_linq import Enumerable
>>> # 3.7 MB
>>> res = Enumerable(range(1000000)).where(lambda x: x>2)
>>> # ... After about 5 seconds pause ...
>>> # 455 MB
>>> for _ in res:
...     pass
... 
>>> # 76 MB
>>> # Total CPU time 9.42 seconds
```

With `asq`:

```
>>> # 3.5 MB
>>> from asq.initiators import query
>>> # 4.1 MB
>>> res = query(range(1000000)).where(lambda x: x>2).select(lambda x: x*x).distinct()
>>> # (instantaneously)
>>> # 4.4 MB
>>> for _ in res:
>>>     pass
>>> # 19.3 MB
>>> # Total CPU time 0.94 seconds
```

Given that `py_linq` is just storing a list internally, you may as well just force evaluation of the result into a list with `asq`:

```
>>> res = query(range(1000000)).where(lambda x: x>2).to_list()
```

As you can see, `asq` is substantially more efficient in terms of both performance and memory than `py_linq`, being about 10x faster and using 1% of the memory on this simple test. Obtaining results multiple times is simply a case of forcing evaluation into a list.  The `asq` packages gives you this choice, whereas `py_linq` will consume resources whether you like it or not.

The great thing is, you can choose which to use.

So from above (with some edits), I **think** this is now the agreed API:

`get` - get file from remote location to `localDir`
`put` - put the file from `localDir` to remote location
`copy_to` - copy a file from wherever seems best to the given directory but keep the `IGangaFile` object the same. Would make use of 'get' code and probably just needs to be implemented in `IGangaFile` as is generic for all.


So my comment above is regarding how best to implement the `copyTo` method. As the code stands in this PR, it changes the `localDir` then call `get` and that's it. I would say it should at least revert `localDir` back given the above. However, I would also argue that it should call `get` (now defined as making sure the file is in `localDir`) and then do a local file copy thus not 'abusing' `get` (though that's a bit strong). It's possible @milliams feels `get` should call `copyTo` but that would involve shifting the actual file retrieval code to `copyTo` instead.

Hopefully that all makes sense 😄

In general, frames are reference counted and can be freely shared between threads (we provide the `frame_queue` primitive to help with that)
The issue with `keep` is not strictly who holds a frame for how long. It is rather **how many** frames can you borrow from the SDK without breaking normal operation. We do everything with fixed pools to avoid per-frame allocations. When you save too many frames in your application, SDK pool will get depleted and you will stop getting new frames. That's where `keep` comes in.

Brain-storming with @jwhui  on how to name the modules/methods, we came up with a proposal/suggestion:

We can define a new set of APIs all starting with `otBorderRouter` for all border router related
OT public functions. Can then add them in a new header file `border_router.h` and their corresponding implementation in `border_router_api.cpp`.  Both of these tied to `OPENTHREAD_ENABLE_BORDER_ROUTER` feature flag).

We can include the following APIs in the new border router module:

- `otBorderRoutetGetLocalNetData()` replacing `otNetDataGetLocal()`
- `otBorderRouterAddOnMeshPrefix()` replacing `otNetDataAddPrefixInfo()` (changes the local network data)
- `otBorderRouterRemoveOnMeshPrefix()` replacing `otNetDataRemovePrefixInfo()` (changes the local network data)
- `otBorderRouterAddRoute()` replacing `otNetDataAddRoute()`.
- `otBorderRouterRemoveRoute()` replacing `otNetDataRemoveRoute()`.
- `otBorderRouterRegisterNetData()` replacing `otNetDataRegister()`.

Two new methods to iterate through local network data on-mesh-prefix and route-prefix
- `otBorderRouterGetNextLocalOnMeshPrefix()`
- `otBorderRouterGetNextLocalRoute()`

We then change the `netdata.h` methods to provide the leader's (partition's) network data only: 

- `otNetDataGet()` to replace `otNetDataGetLeader()`
- `otNetDataGetNextOnMeshPrefix()` to replace `otNetDataGetNextPrefixInfo()` without having `aLocal` parameter and always using the leader's net data.
- `otNetDataGetNextRoute()` to replace `otNetDataGetNextRoute()` without having `aLocal` parameter and always using leader's net data.
- `otNetDataGetVersion()` (as is)
- `otNetDataGetStableVersion()` (as is)

@yarharel Can you enable debug log and capture the output when this happens to aid debug of this?  After you construct your client you need to override the detault logging level

```python
client = ibmiotf.gateway.Client(options)
client.logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
```

There are scenarios where the client will not auto-reconnect, and raise a `ConnectionException` back up to the user to handle so that they can make a decision about how to proceed:
- if we don't connect in 30 seconds we stop & raise an exception
- if we hit a socket error we stop & raise an exception

Are you seeing one of these exceptions raised, which one is it?

At the moment, the expectation is on the user of the library to handle the raised `ConnectionException` in their own code if either of these two scenarios occur.

In the samples we have developed using the library that is where something like supervisord or runit comes into play, the library will "swallow" most network glitches/minor disconnects, but occasionally a ConnectionException will be raised, which causes the program to exit, but the supervisor restarts it automatically.

Of course, it would be just as valid to handle ConnectionException in the python code itself in a loop, but I feel it's a cleaner approach letting the program die and having something like supervisord/runit handle lifecycle around restarts etc.

@zeeshansayyed The minority class is the class with the less samples. Since that you are repeating several time the procedure, the algorithm is taking the class with minimum sample at time $t$

Taking your example:
```python
0:  (10413, 2500) (10413,) Counter({1.0: 3763, 3.0: 3763, 4.0: 2365, 2.0: 522})
1:  (13654, 2500) (13654,) Counter({1.0: 3763, 2.0: 3763, 3.0: 3763, 4.0: 2365})
2:  (15052, 2500) (15052,) Counter({1.0: 3763, 2.0: 3763, 3.0: 3763, 4.0: 3763})
3:  (15052, 2500) (15052,) Counter({1.0: 3763, 2.0: 3763, 3.0: 3763, 4.0: 3763})
4:  (15052, 2500) (15052,) Counter({1.0: 3763, 2.0: 3763, 3.0: 3763, 4.0: 3763})
```

Class 1 or 3 was the minority at first. At the second iteration, class 2 was the minority and got up-sampled. At the third, the class 4 was up-sampled since it was the minority. Then, nothing actually happened, it just returned the same samples.

Hmm, I initially thought `.astype()` should always return a copy (which is also a default behaviour in NumPy). However, in Python API `np.array.astype` also accepts an optional `copy` argument:

```
copy : bool, optional
    By default, astype always returns a newly allocated array. If this
    is set to false, and the `dtype`, `order`, and `subok`
    requirements are satisfied, the input array is returned instead
    of a copy.
```

Maybe we could do something similar; because we're not changing `order` (F/C) and `subok`, it's actually [sufficient](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/numpy/core/src/multiarray/methods.c#L801) to just set the casting policy to `equiv`. NumPy just increfs the original array and returns you that; we'd have to also wrap it `array_t`. Then you could potentially do:

```cpp
ptr = arr.astype<T>(casting::equiv, false).data()
```

It turns out that pykube uses requests, which pulls in urllib3, which attempts to pull in match_hostname first from Python native SSL, then from backports, falling back on its own implementation if neither are available. However, the latest backports's match_hostname seems to be more complete than the native SSL match_hostname, allowing for IP addresses. You can monkey-patch this in your end script with the following, so long as "IP Address:<IP address>" actually does show up in subjectAltNames:

```python
import backports.ssl_match_hostname
import pykube

# Monkey-patch match_hostname with backports's match_hostname, allowing for IP addresses
# XXX: the exception that this might raise is backports.ssl_match_hostname.CertificateError
pykube.http.requests.packages.urllib3.connection.match_hostname = backports.ssl_match_hostname.match_hostname
```

For example, if we want to transfer a matrix from `Octave` to `SoS`, using `save(fullfile(tempdir, 'mat2py.mat'), 'obj');`, which works in `Matlab`, would also works in `Octave`, but the `.mat` file created by `Octave` isn't readable. Instead, we should use `save -6 mat2py.mat` in `Octave`. However, this function doesn't contain the location parameter, so we simply need to change the working directory (use cd(tempdir)) before save the `.mat` file. 
Also, it seems `isstr' is going to be removed from `Octave`, and we will use `ischar` to replace it.

All in all, as Dr. Peng said, we have to separate .m files for Matlab and Octave.

What about special transformation for both imputs and targets ? This may create some duplicates functions like randomcrop for image based target (maybe add target as an optional argument ?) , but i don't see how we would apply properly coherent transformations for input and target.

We could also give a seed as an argument in addition to img in __getitem__ , but we are not garanteed `transform` and `target_transform` will be constructed in a dual manner such that random variables will have coherent effects (like flipping both inputs and targets, or translating BB coordinates according to input img transformation)

I feel like there should 3 types of transform : `transform_input` that deals with transformations that are independent of target, like flip-crop for classification, `transform_target` idem for target and lastly `co_transform`(sorry about bad terminology) that deals with dependent transformations and must take input and target as arguments and I believe concerns the vast majority of data augmentation for more geometric problems, such as detection, depth estimation, flow, etc

I chatted more with Philipp today, and here are my preferences:

(a) Either A or B + adding remote to methods, i.e., class.method.remote(). I know this is verbose but it is clear and consistent. If we tell people that we use f.remote() because we want to make them aware that a remote function returns an object_id, then we should do the same for methods. Otherwise, we might create confusion. 

(b) D, again because it solves (2) as well

(c) Either A or B; this is last because it doesn't solve (2)

All of the above are much better than what we have today, so one thing I want to make sure is that we **pick one and go for it before the release**. We do not want to get into one of these cases where the "perfect to be the enemy of good" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perfect_is_the_enemy_of_good)

I'm sorry about that slowness. However, the bgen format itself was designed to be fast for selecting genotypes specifically. It stores a chunk of all the samples for a given genotype, in a compressed way. This means that selecting the samples for a given genotype requires to load and uncompress the entire file. This is a limitation of bgen format itself.

If that operation is necessary, I recommend you to save into another file format (hdf5, for example) by reading genotype by genotype.

> the middle button should be twice the size of the outer buttons.

I think the confusion is that's not what the text "items flex proportional to their weight" means. I'd encourage you to read the flexbox tutorial pointed out in that documentation page (https://css-tricks.com/snippets/css/a-guide-to-flexbox/), as it's a bit subtle. Basically, it's going to take the space left over by the widgets and divide that leftover space in proportion to the weights. It's not about stretching the widgets to be twice the size of other widgets.

> Honestly, forgetting to put an await on things is not something that I've lost a lot of sleep thinking about. Python already provides a warning message about unawaited coroutines. And if you forget the await, it is usually readily apparent that something is quite wrong in other ways. 

Yeah, this has been my experience too. People do forget to put `await` sometimes when they begin writing async code, but usually understand why the program doesn't work pretty quickly.

> One feature that'd be really useful from the Curio front would be some way to know whether a callable has been triggered from within the context of a coroutine or not.

You can use `sys.set_coroutine_wrapper` to wrap all coroutines into some awaitable object that enables the tracking. Would only recommend to do that for some debug mode.
